Skip to content Suggested Topics: Home Top Pharma Strategy Roche, a company built on oncology and diagnostics reinventing its pipeline Photo credits: Franck V Newsletter Signup - Under Article / In Page " * " indicates required fields There was a time when the numbers told an unoptimistic story about Roche. Its three oncology blockbusters, Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, had built it into the world’s largest cancer drug maker, collectively generating around $21 billion in annual sales at their peak. Then, biosimilars arrived. Deutsche Bank warned that Roche had $22 billion in revenues exposed to competition. In 2018, JP Morgan analysts forecast double-digit declines for its biggest products. When the erosion hit, it came harder and faster than Roche’s projections had anticipated; the $9.6 billion gap the company had modeled between 2018 and 2023 sales was crossed by 2021. But Roche grew through it, as a new generation of medicines – Ocrevus in multiple sclerosis, Hemlibra in hemophilia, and Vabysmo in retinal disease – absorbed the hit. By 2025, its top five growth drivers were pulling in over $20 billion in combined sales. But what has Roche chosen to do next? Beyond consolidating around its existing strengths, the company has launched a second strategic reinvention: entering obesity, placing a $7.1 billion bet on a novel inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) mechanism, and acquiring an allogeneic cell therapy platform, all within roughly two years. Table of contents What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche What the Roche we know today was built on Back to Top The company that exists today is the product of two decisions made decades apart. In 1990, Roche acquired a 60% stake in the California biotech Genentech. The full buyout came in 2009, when Roche paid $46.8 billion for the remaining publicly held shares, at the time the largest acquisition in Swiss corporate history. The acquisition wasn’t as straightforward as it may seem. When Roche moved to buy out Genentech’s minority shareholders in July 2008, the initial offer of $89 per share triggered an immediate shareholder backlash. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging breach of fiduciary duty, with plaintiffs arguing Roche had deliberately timed the bid to exploit market turmoil and a weakened dollar. The litigation was eventually settled, but not before Roche was forced to raise its bid to $95 per share. For that price, Roche got more than just a drug portfolio; it got the platform that produced Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, the oncology trio that would come to define the company’s commercial identity for the next two decades. Herceptin changed HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Avastin became a standard of care across multiple tumor types. Rituxan dominated blood cancer and later moved into autoimmune disease. They established Roche as the world’s largest oncology company, a position it held for years on the back of medicines that came almost entirely from Genentech’s labs. The other defining decision came earlier, in 1997, when Roche acquired Boehringer Mannheim for $11 billion. It gave Roche a diagnostics business to sit alongside its pharma business, at a time when most of its competitors were treating diagnostics as a secondary concern. The logic was that if you can develop the targeted therapy and the diagnostic test that identifies which patients will respond to it, you control both ends of the treatment decision. This became the foundation of what Roche calls personalized healthcare, a concept the company has used to frame its strategy for decades. The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion Back to Top In September 2019, Bill Anderson, CEO of Roche Pharmaceutical Division at the time, was estimating 60 to 70% erosion across Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, which he called a “conservative” projection. The company was looking at a CHF 9.6 billion ($11.8 billion) sales gap between 2018 and 2023. The dynamic was different from a conventional small-molecule patent cliff. Biosimilars to biologics face a slower and more complex uptake due to physician inertia, switching costs, and payer dynamics, all of which slow the erosion compared to generic drugs. Still, by Q1 2022, Avastin had shed 32% of its sales year-on-year, Herceptin 19%, and Rituxan 21%. On one side, Roche operated a deliberate pipeline rationalization: in early 2024, the company cut eight early- to mid-stage candidates. Five from neurology and three in oncology and hematology were discontinued as part of what CEO Thomas Schinecker described as a reallocation of resources toward programs with the clearest differentiation. On the other side, Roche made partnerships and acquisitions of de-risked external assets, with Schinecker stating that Roche was looking to acquire drug assets at all stages of development. The new blockbusters that filled the gap Back to Top In 2025, Roche’s new five main growth drivers, Ocrevus, Hemlibra, Vabysmo, Phesgo, and Xolair, generated combined sales of CHF 21.4 billion ($26.4 billion), up CHF 3.2 billion ($3.9 billion) from 2024. Ocrevus, an anti-CD20 antibody for multiple sclerosis approved in 2017, is Roche’s biggest seller at CHF 7.01 billion ($8.6 billion) in 2025. Hemlibra, a bispecific antibody for hemophilia A, reached CHF 4.8 billion ($5.9 billion), up 11% year-on-year. Vabysmo, a dual-targeting antibody for retinal diseases, launched in early 2022 and became one of Roche’s fastest-growing products. Suggested Articles How Daiichi Sankyo aims to become a top five oncology player by 2035 Blockbuster sales, lawsuits, and approvals: is there a recovery in sight for Bayer in 2026? Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant The company made cautious bets in areas where it had expertise. Ocrevus extended Roche’s existing antibody expertise into a new indication. Hemlibra and Vabysmo both used the bispecific antibody format, a platform Roche had been developing internally for years. Phesgo was a lifecycle management of the HER2 franchise. Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche Back to Top Since 2023, Roche has deployed significant capital towards dealmaking. At the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in January 2025, Roche Pharmaceutical Division’s CEO Teresa Graham said Roche has roughly $10 billion in M&A firepower it can deploy each year, and is ready to use it “if and when we find transformative assets that are either complementary to our portfolio or change the game in important diseases.” Across different therapeutic areas, two types dominate in Roche’s dealmaking strategy. The first is large-scale acquisitions of late-stage or phase-3-ready assets where the clinical risk is substantially de-risked. First, Telavant in October 2023 for $7.1 billion upfront , plus a $150 million near-term milestone, giving Roche U.S. and Japan rights to afimkibart, an anti-TL1A antibody with strong phase 2b data in ulcerative colitis and a novel mechanism targeting both inflammation and fibrosis. Six weeks later, Carmot Therapeutics for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, bringing three clinical-stage obesity candidates, including CT-388, a phase-2-ready dual GLP-1/GIP agonist. Then, 89bio in September 2025, $2.4 billion upfront and up to $3.5 billion total, for pegozafermin, an FGF21 analog in phase 3 for MASH. The second deal type is smaller and more targeted. Poseida Therapeutics in November 2024 for up to $1.5 billion, bringing an allogeneic CAR-T platform built on non-viral, T stem cell memory-rich engineering, a technology Roche had already been partnering with since 2022. Regor Therapeutics in September 2024 for $850 million upfront, technically an asset purchase through Genentech, bringing two next-generation CDK inhibitors for breast cancer, positioned as potential successors to the CDK4/6 inhibitor class. And in March 2025, the Zealand Pharma collaboration : $1.65 billion upfront against a total potential value of $5.3 billion to co-develop petrelintide, an amylin analog being positioned as a combination partner for CT-388 in obesity. These deals represent commitments of well over $15 billion in upfront capital across roughly two years, in IBD, obesity, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), cell therapy, and breast cancer oncology. How is Roche strategically positioned? Back to Top The most ambitious expansion Roche has operated in the past few years might be towards obesity. But it has also invested significantly in immunology, as well as neuroscience. Roche’s obesity pipeline: the most audacious bet Roche’s obesity pipeline strategy looks like a portfolio bet on where the market will be in five to seven years. The lead asset is CT-388, a once-weekly injectable dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist, the same mechanistic class as Eli Lilly’s Zepbound. In January 2026, Roche published phase 2 data showing placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.5% at 48 weeks at the highest dose tested, without reaching a plateau. Two phase 3 trials are now underway, initiated in Q1 2026. The timing might not be the most convenient, as CT-388 is entering phase 3 at a point when Wegovy and Zepbound already have years of commercial infrastructure, physician familiarity, and patient uptake behind them. Even if the phase 3 trials deliver, Roche is behind its competition. Roche’s answer to that timing problem is the combination strategy. The obesity market today is dominated by single-agent GLP-1s, but their key limitation is tolerability, with nausea driving most discontinuation, and concerns about muscle mass loss on long-term treatment. Roche is betting that by the time it arrives, combinations addressing those limitations will be the new standard. Petrelintide, acquired via the $1.65 billion upfront Zealand Pharma collaboration, is an amylin analog designed to complement GLP-1 therapy by reducing nausea and preserving lean mass, with a combination study with CT-388 planned for the first half of 2026. However, not all bets can go all the way, and in March 2026, Roche announced it would discontinue Emugrobart, an anti-myostatin antibody meant to address lean mass preservation that was tested in combination with Mounjaro. Bowel disease and immunology: inside Roche’s $7 billion bet on TL1A IBD is a large and commercially active market, but it remains one where current therapies fall short of what patients need. Despite a generation of biologics, TNF inhibitors, IL-12/23 antibodies, and integrin blockers, most patients with ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease do not achieve lasting remission. This is what made TL1A, a cytokine involved in both inflammation and fibrosis, one of the most closely watched targets in immunology over the past few years. TL1A is distinct from most existing IBD targets because it drives immune activation in addition to fibrosis, the scarring that causes long-term complications in Crohn’s disease. An antibody that addresses both processes could do more than current biologics. That is the bet behind afimkibart, the anti-TL1A antibody Roche acquired with Telavant. Phase 3 trials in ulcerative colitis are ongoing, with a regulatory filing expected in 2027, and a phase 2 in Crohn’s disease also underway. However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once. The timing might not be the most convenient, as CT-388 is entering phase 3 at a point when Wegovy and Zepbound already have years of commercial infrastructure, physician familiarity, and patient uptake behind them. Even if the phase 3 trials deliver, Roche is behind its competition. Roche’s answer to that timing problem is the combination strategy. The obesity market today is dominated by single-agent GLP-1s, but their key limitation is tolerability, with nausea driving most discontinuation, and concerns about muscle mass loss on long-term treatment. Roche is betting that by the time it arrives, combinations addressing those limitations will be the new standard. Petrelintide, acquired via the $1.65 billion upfront Zealand Pharma collaboration, is an amylin analog designed to complement GLP-1 therapy by reducing nausea and preserving lean mass, with a combination study with CT-388 planned for the first half of 2026. However, not all bets can go all the way, and in March 2026, Roche announced it would discontinue Emugrobart, an anti-myostatin antibody meant to address lean mass preservation that was tested in combination with Mounjaro. Bowel disease and immunology: inside Roche’s $7 billion bet on TL1A IBD is a large and commercially active market, but it remains one where current therapies fall short of what patients need. Despite a generation of biologics, TNF inhibitors, IL-12/23 antibodies, and integrin blockers, most patients with ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease do not achieve lasting remission. This is what made TL1A, a cytokine involved in both inflammation and fibrosis, one of the most closely watched targets in immunology over the past few years. TL1A is distinct from most existing IBD targets because it drives immune activation in addition to fibrosis, the scarring that causes long-term complications in Crohn’s disease. An antibody that addresses both processes could do more than current biologics. That is the bet behind afimkibart, the anti-TL1A antibody Roche acquired with Telavant. Phase 3 trials in ulcerative colitis are ongoing, with a regulatory filing expected in 2027, and a phase 2 in Crohn’s disease also underway. However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once. Top Pharma Strategy
Roche, a company built on oncology and diagnostics reinventing its pipeline
Photo credits: Franck V Newsletter Signup - Under Article / In Page " * " indicates required fields There was a time when the numbers told an unoptimistic story about Roche. Its three oncology blockbusters, Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, had built it into the world’s largest cancer drug maker, collectively generating around $21 billion in annual sales at their peak. Then, biosimilars arrived. Deutsche Bank warned that Roche had $22 billion in revenues exposed to competition. In 2018, JP Morgan analysts forecast double-digit declines for its biggest products. When the erosion hit, it came harder and faster than Roche’s projections had anticipated; the $9.6 billion gap the company had modeled between 2018 and 2023 sales was crossed by 2021. But Roche grew through it, as a new generation of medicines – Ocrevus in multiple sclerosis, Hemlibra in hemophilia, and Vabysmo in retinal disease – absorbed the hit. By 2025, its top five growth drivers were pulling in over $20 billion in combined sales. But what has Roche chosen to do next? Beyond consolidating around its existing strengths, the company has launched a second strategic reinvention: entering obesity, placing a $7.1 billion bet on a novel inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) mechanism, and acquiring an allogeneic cell therapy platform, all within roughly two years. Table of contents What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche What the Roche we know today was built on Back to Top The company that exists today is the product of two decisions made decades apart. In 1990, Roche acquired a 60% stake in the California biotech Genentech. The full buyout came in 2009, when Roche paid $46.8 billion for the remaining publicly held shares, at the time the largest acquisition in Swiss corporate history. The acquisition wasn’t as straightforward as it may seem. When Roche moved to buy out Genentech’s minority shareholders in July 2008, the initial offer of $89 per share triggered an immediate shareholder backlash. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging breach of fiduciary duty, with plaintiffs arguing Roche had deliberately timed the bid to exploit market turmoil and a weakened dollar. The litigation was eventually settled, but not before Roche was forced to raise its bid to $95 per share. For that price, Roche got more than just a drug portfolio; it got the platform that produced Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, the oncology trio that would come to define the company’s commercial identity for the next two decades. Herceptin changed HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Avastin became a standard of care across multiple tumor types. Rituxan dominated blood cancer and later moved into autoimmune disease. They established Roche as the world’s largest oncology company, a position it held for years on the back of medicines that came almost entirely from Genentech’s labs. The other defining decision came earlier, in 1997, when Roche acquired Boehringer Mannheim for $11 billion. It gave Roche a diagnostics business to sit alongside its pharma business, at a time when most of its competitors were treating diagnostics as a secondary concern. The logic was that if you can develop the targeted therapy and the diagnostic test that identifies which patients will respond to it, you control both ends of the treatment decision. This became the foundation of what Roche calls personalized healthcare, a concept the company has used to frame its strategy for decades. The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion Back to Top In September 2019, Bill Anderson, CEO of Roche Pharmaceutical Division at the time, was estimating 60 to 70% erosion across Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, which he called a “conservative” projection. The company was looking at a CHF 9.6 billion ($11.8 billion) sales gap between 2018 and 2023. The dynamic was different from a conventional small-molecule patent cliff. Biosimilars to biologics face a slower and more complex uptake due to physician inertia, switching costs, and payer dynamics, all of which slow the erosion compared to generic drugs. Still, by Q1 2022, Avastin had shed 32% of its sales year-on-year, Herceptin 19%, and Rituxan 21%. On one side, Roche operated a deliberate pipeline rationalization: in early 2024, the company cut eight early- to mid-stage candidates. Five from neurology and three in oncology and hematology were discontinued as part of what CEO Thomas Schinecker described as a reallocation of resources toward programs with the clearest differentiation. On the other side, Roche made partnerships and acquisitions of de-risked external assets, with Schinecker stating that Roche was looking to acquire drug assets at all stages of development. The new blockbusters that filled the gap Back to Top In 2025, Roche’s new five main growth drivers, Ocrevus, Hemlibra, Vabysmo, Phesgo, and Xolair, generated combined sales of CHF 21.4 billion ($26.4 billion), up CHF 3.2 billion ($3.9 billion) from 2024. Ocrevus, an anti-CD20 antibody for multiple sclerosis approved in 2017, is Roche’s biggest seller at CHF 7.01 billion ($8.6 billion) in 2025. Hemlibra, a bispecific antibody for hemophilia A, reached CHF 4.8 billion ($5.9 billion), up 11% year-on-year. Vabysmo, a dual-targeting antibody for retinal diseases, launched in early 2022 and became one of Roche’s fastest-growing products. Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant The company made cautious bets in areas where it had expertise. Ocrevus extended Roche’s existing antibody expertise into a new indication. Hemlibra and Vabysmo both used the bispecific antibody format, a platform Roche had been developing internally for years. Phesgo was a lifecycle management of the HER2 franchise. Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche Back to Top Since 2023, Roche has deployed significant capital towards dealmaking. At the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in January 2025, Roche Pharmaceutical Division’s CEO Teresa Graham said Roche has roughly $10 billion in M&A firepower it can deploy each year, and is ready to use it “if and when we find transformative assets that are either complementary to our portfolio or change the game in important diseases.” Across different therapeutic areas, two types dominate in Roche’s dealmaking strategy. The first is large-scale acquisitions of late-stage or phase-3-ready assets where the clinical risk is substantially de-risked. First, Telavant in October 2023 for $7.1 billion upfront , plus a $150 million near-term milestone, giving Roche U.S. and Japan rights to afimkibart, an anti-TL1A antibody with strong phase 2b data in ulcerative colitis and a novel mechanism targeting both inflammation and fibrosis. Six weeks later, Carmot Therapeutics for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, bringing three clinical-stage obesity candidates, including CT-388, a phase-2-ready dual GLP-1/GIP agonist. Then, 89bio in September 2025, $2.4 billion upfront and up to $3.5 billion total, for pegozafermin, an FGF21 analog in phase 3 for MASH. The second deal type is smaller and more targeted. Poseida Therapeutics in November 2024 for up to $1.5 billion, bringing an allogeneic CAR-T platform built on non-viral, T stem cell memory-rich engineering, a technology Roche had already been partnering with since 2022. Regor Therapeutics in September 2024 for $850 million upfront, technically an asset purchase through Genentech, bringing two next-generation CDK inhibitors for breast cancer, positioned as potential successors to the CDK4/6 inhibitor class. And in March 2025, the Zealand Pharma collaboration : $1.65 billion upfront against a total potential value of $5.3 billion to co-develop petrelintide, an amylin analog being positioned as a combination partner for CT-388 in obesity. These deals represent commitments of well over $15 billion in upfront capital across roughly two years, in IBD, obesity, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), cell therapy, and breast cancer oncology. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once. Explore other topics: Obesity Roche ADVERTISEMENT

Its three oncology blockbusters, Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, had built it into the world’s largest cancer drug maker, collectively generating around $21 billion in annual sales at their peak. Then, biosimilars arrived. Deutsche Bank warned that Roche had $22 billion in revenues exposed to competition. In 2018, JP Morgan analysts forecast double-digit declines for its biggest products. When the erosion hit, it came harder and faster than Roche’s projections had anticipated; the $9.6 billion gap the company had modeled between 2018 and 2023 sales was crossed by 2021. But Roche grew through it, as a new generation of medicines – Ocrevus in multiple sclerosis, Hemlibra in hemophilia, and Vabysmo in retinal disease – absorbed the hit. By 2025, its top five growth drivers were pulling in over $20 billion in combined sales. But what has Roche chosen to do next? Beyond consolidating around its existing strengths, the company has launched a second strategic reinvention: entering obesity, placing a $7.1 billion bet on a novel inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) mechanism, and acquiring an allogeneic cell therapy platform, all within roughly two years. Table of contents What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche What the Roche we know today was built on Back to Top The company that exists today is the product of two decisions made decades apart. In 1990, Roche acquired a 60% stake in the California biotech Genentech. The full buyout came in 2009, when Roche paid $46.8 billion for the remaining publicly held shares, at the time the largest acquisition in Swiss corporate history. The acquisition wasn’t as straightforward as it may seem. When Roche moved to buy out Genentech’s minority shareholders in July 2008, the initial offer of $89 per share triggered an immediate shareholder backlash. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging breach of fiduciary duty, with plaintiffs arguing Roche had deliberately timed the bid to exploit market turmoil and a weakened dollar. The litigation was eventually settled, but not before Roche was forced to raise its bid to $95 per share. For that price, Roche got more than just a drug portfolio; it got the platform that produced Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, the oncology trio that would come to define the company’s commercial identity for the next two decades. Herceptin changed HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Avastin became a standard of care across multiple tumor types. Rituxan dominated blood cancer and later moved into autoimmune disease. They established Roche as the world’s largest oncology company, a position it held for years on the back of medicines that came almost entirely from Genentech’s labs. The other defining decision came earlier, in 1997, when Roche acquired Boehringer Mannheim for $11 billion. It gave Roche a diagnostics business to sit alongside its pharma business, at a time when most of its competitors were treating diagnostics as a secondary concern. The logic was that if you can develop the targeted therapy and the diagnostic test that identifies which patients will respond to it, you control both ends of the treatment decision. This became the foundation of what Roche calls personalized healthcare, a concept the company has used to frame its strategy for decades. The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion Back to Top In September 2019, Bill Anderson, CEO of Roche Pharmaceutical Division at the time, was estimating 60 to 70% erosion across Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, which he called a “conservative” projection. The company was looking at a CHF 9.6 billion ($11.8 billion) sales gap between 2018 and 2023. The dynamic was different from a conventional small-molecule patent cliff. Biosimilars to biologics face a slower and more complex uptake due to physician inertia, switching costs, and payer dynamics, all of which slow the erosion compared to generic drugs. Still, by Q1 2022, Avastin had shed 32% of its sales year-on-year, Herceptin 19%, and Rituxan 21%. On one side, Roche operated a deliberate pipeline rationalization: in early 2024, the company cut eight early- to mid-stage candidates. Five from neurology and three in oncology and hematology were discontinued as part of what CEO Thomas Schinecker described as a reallocation of resources toward programs with the clearest differentiation. On the other side, Roche made partnerships and acquisitions of de-risked external assets, with Schinecker stating that Roche was looking to acquire drug assets at all stages of development. The new blockbusters that filled the gap Back to Top In 2025, Roche’s new five main growth drivers, Ocrevus, Hemlibra, Vabysmo, Phesgo, and Xolair, generated combined sales of CHF 21.4 billion ($26.4 billion), up CHF 3.2 billion ($3.9 billion) from 2024. Ocrevus, an anti-CD20 antibody for multiple sclerosis approved in 2017, is Roche’s biggest seller at CHF 7.01 billion ($8.6 billion) in 2025. Hemlibra, a bispecific antibody for hemophilia A, reached CHF 4.8 billion ($5.9 billion), up 11% year-on-year. Vabysmo, a dual-targeting antibody for retinal diseases, launched in early 2022 and became one of Roche’s fastest-growing products. Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant The company made cautious bets in areas where it had expertise. Ocrevus extended Roche’s existing antibody expertise into a new indication. Hemlibra and Vabysmo both used the bispecific antibody format, a platform Roche had been developing internally for years. Phesgo was a lifecycle management of the HER2 franchise. Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche Back to Top Since 2023, Roche has deployed significant capital towards dealmaking. At the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in January 2025, Roche Pharmaceutical Division’s CEO Teresa Graham said Roche has roughly $10 billion in M&A firepower it can deploy each year, and is ready to use it “if and when we find transformative assets that are either complementary to our portfolio or change the game in important diseases.” Across different therapeutic areas, two types dominate in Roche’s dealmaking strategy. The first is large-scale acquisitions of late-stage or phase-3-ready assets where the clinical risk is substantially de-risked. First, Telavant in October 2023 for $7.1 billion upfront , plus a $150 million near-term milestone, giving Roche U.S. and Japan rights to afimkibart, an anti-TL1A antibody with strong phase 2b data in ulcerative colitis and a novel mechanism targeting both inflammation and fibrosis. Six weeks later, Carmot Therapeutics for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, bringing three clinical-stage obesity candidates, including CT-388, a phase-2-ready dual GLP-1/GIP agonist. Then, 89bio in September 2025, $2.4 billion upfront and up to $3.5 billion total, for pegozafermin, an FGF21 analog in phase 3 for MASH. The second deal type is smaller and more targeted. Poseida Therapeutics in November 2024 for up to $1.5 billion, bringing an allogeneic CAR-T platform built on non-viral, T stem cell memory-rich engineering, a technology Roche had already been partnering with since 2022. Regor Therapeutics in September 2024 for $850 million upfront, technically an asset purchase through Genentech, bringing two next-generation CDK inhibitors for breast cancer, positioned as potential successors to the CDK4/6 inhibitor class. And in March 2025, the Zealand Pharma collaboration : $1.65 billion upfront against a total potential value of $5.3 billion to co-develop petrelintide, an amylin analog being positioned as a combination partner for CT-388 in obesity. These deals represent commitments of well over $15 billion in upfront capital across roughly two years, in IBD, obesity, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), cell therapy, and breast cancer oncology. How is Roche strategically positioned? Back to Top The most ambitious expansion Roche has operated in the past few years might be towards obesity. But it has also invested significantly in immunology, as well as neuroscience. Roche’s obesity pipeline: the most audacious bet Roche’s obesity pipeline strategy looks like a portfolio bet on where the market will be in five to seven years. The lead asset is CT-388, a once-weekly injectable dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist, the same mechanistic class as Eli Lilly’s Zepbound. In January 2026, Roche published phase 2 data showing placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.5% at 48 weeks at the highest dose tested, without reaching a plateau. Two phase 3 trials are now underway, initiated in Q1 2026. The timing might not be the most convenient, as CT-388 is entering phase 3 at a point when Wegovy and Zepbound already have years of commercial infrastructure, physician familiarity, and patient uptake behind them. Even if the phase 3 trials deliver, Roche is behind its competition. Roche’s answer to that timing problem is the combination strategy. The obesity market today is dominated by single-agent GLP-1s, but their key limitation is tolerability, with nausea driving most discontinuation, and concerns about muscle mass loss on long-term treatment. Roche is betting that by the time it arrives, combinations addressing those limitations will be the new standard. Petrelintide, acquired via the $1.65 billion upfront Zealand Pharma collaboration, is an amylin analog designed to complement GLP-1 therapy by reducing nausea and preserving lean mass, with a combination study with CT-388 planned for the first half of 2026. However, not all bets can go all the way, and in March 2026, Roche announced it would discontinue Emugrobart, an anti-myostatin antibody meant to address lean mass preservation that was tested in combination with Mounjaro. Bowel disease and immunology: inside Roche’s $7 billion bet on TL1A IBD is a large and commercially active market, but it remains one where current therapies fall short of what patients need. Despite a generation of biologics, TNF inhibitors, IL-12/23 antibodies, and integrin blockers, most patients with ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease do not achieve lasting remission. This is what made TL1A, a cytokine involved in both inflammation and fibrosis, one of the most closely watched targets in immunology over the past few years. TL1A is distinct from most existing IBD targets because it drives immune activation in addition to fibrosis, the scarring that causes long-term complications in Crohn’s disease. An antibody that addresses both processes could do more than current biologics. That is the bet behind afimkibart, the anti-TL1A antibody Roche acquired with Telavant. Phase 3 trials in ulcerative colitis are ongoing, with a regulatory filing expected in 2027, and a phase 2 in Crohn’s disease also underway. However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before. Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest. On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before. Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not. The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for. Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once.
There was a time when the numbers told an unoptimistic story about Roche. Its three oncology blockbusters, Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, had built it into the world’s largest cancer drug maker, collectively generating around $21 billion in annual sales at their peak. Then, biosimilars arrived. Deutsche Bank warned that Roche had $22 billion in revenues exposed to competition. In 2018, JP Morgan analysts forecast double-digit declines for its biggest products. When the erosion hit, it came harder and faster than Roche’s projections had anticipated; the $9.6 billion gap the company had modeled between 2018 and 2023 sales was crossed by 2021.
forecast
crossed
But Roche grew through it, as a new generation of medicines – Ocrevus in multiple sclerosis, Hemlibra in hemophilia, and Vabysmo in retinal disease – absorbed the hit. By 2025, its top five growth drivers were pulling in over $20 billion in combined sales.
pulling
But what has Roche chosen to do next? Beyond consolidating around its existing strengths, the company has launched a second strategic reinvention: entering obesity, placing a $7.1 billion bet on a novel inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) mechanism, and acquiring an allogeneic cell therapy platform, all within roughly two years.
inflammatory bowel disease
Table of contents What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche
Table of contents
What the Roche we know today was built on The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion The new blockbusters that filled the gap Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche How is Roche strategically positioned? A second reinvention for Roche
What the Roche we know today was built on
The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion
The new blockbusters that filled the gap
Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche
How is Roche strategically positioned?
A second reinvention for Roche
What the Roche we know today was built on Back to Top
Back to Top
The company that exists today is the product of two decisions made decades apart. In 1990, Roche acquired a 60% stake in the California biotech Genentech. The full buyout came in 2009, when Roche paid $46.8 billion for the remaining publicly held shares, at the time the largest acquisition in Swiss corporate history.
The acquisition wasn’t as straightforward as it may seem. When Roche moved to buy out Genentech’s minority shareholders in July 2008, the initial offer of $89 per share triggered an immediate shareholder backlash. A class action lawsuit was filed alleging breach of fiduciary duty, with plaintiffs arguing Roche had deliberately timed the bid to exploit market turmoil and a weakened dollar. The litigation was eventually settled, but not before Roche was forced to raise its bid to $95 per share.
filed
For that price, Roche got more than just a drug portfolio; it got the platform that produced Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, the oncology trio that would come to define the company’s commercial identity for the next two decades.
Herceptin changed HER2-positive breast cancer treatment. Avastin became a standard of care across multiple tumor types. Rituxan dominated blood cancer and later moved into autoimmune disease. They established Roche as the world’s largest oncology company, a position it held for years on the back of medicines that came almost entirely from Genentech’s labs.
The other defining decision came earlier, in 1997, when Roche acquired Boehringer Mannheim for $11 billion. It gave Roche a diagnostics business to sit alongside its pharma business, at a time when most of its competitors were treating diagnostics as a secondary concern. The logic was that if you can develop the targeted therapy and the diagnostic test that identifies which patients will respond to it, you control both ends of the treatment decision.
acquired
This became the foundation of what Roche calls personalized healthcare, a concept the company has used to frame its strategy for decades.
The Roche crisis: biosimilar erosion Back to Top
In September 2019, Bill Anderson, CEO of Roche Pharmaceutical Division at the time, was estimating 60 to 70% erosion across Herceptin, Avastin, and Rituxan, which he called a “conservative” projection. The company was looking at a CHF 9.6 billion ($11.8 billion) sales gap between 2018 and 2023.
looking
The dynamic was different from a conventional small-molecule patent cliff. Biosimilars to biologics face a slower and more complex uptake due to physician inertia, switching costs, and payer dynamics, all of which slow the erosion compared to generic drugs. Still, by Q1 2022, Avastin had shed 32% of its sales year-on-year, Herceptin 19%, and Rituxan 21%.
had shed
On one side, Roche operated a deliberate pipeline rationalization: in early 2024, the company cut eight early- to mid-stage candidates. Five from neurology and three in oncology and hematology were discontinued as part of what CEO Thomas Schinecker described as a reallocation of resources toward programs with the clearest differentiation.
cut
On the other side, Roche made partnerships and acquisitions of de-risked external assets, with Schinecker stating that Roche was looking to acquire drug assets at all stages of development.
The new blockbusters that filled the gap Back to Top
In 2025, Roche’s new five main growth drivers, Ocrevus, Hemlibra, Vabysmo, Phesgo, and Xolair, generated combined sales of CHF 21.4 billion ($26.4 billion), up CHF 3.2 billion ($3.9 billion) from 2024.
generated
Ocrevus, an anti-CD20 antibody for multiple sclerosis approved in 2017, is Roche’s biggest seller at CHF 7.01 billion ($8.6 billion) in 2025. Hemlibra, a bispecific antibody for hemophilia A, reached CHF 4.8 billion ($5.9 billion), up 11% year-on-year. Vabysmo, a dual-targeting antibody for retinal diseases, launched in early 2022 and became one of Roche’s fastest-growing products.
Suggested Articles How Daiichi Sankyo aims to become a top five oncology player by 2035 Blockbuster sales, lawsuits, and approvals: is there a recovery in sight for Bayer in 2026? Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant
Suggested Articles
How Daiichi Sankyo aims to become a top five oncology player by 2035 Blockbuster sales, lawsuits, and approvals: is there a recovery in sight for Bayer in 2026? Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant
How Daiichi Sankyo aims to become a top five oncology player by 2035
Blockbuster sales, lawsuits, and approvals: is there a recovery in sight for Bayer in 2026?
Inside CSL’s strategy to simplify a biotech giant
The company made cautious bets in areas where it had expertise. Ocrevus extended Roche’s existing antibody expertise into a new indication. Hemlibra and Vabysmo both used the bispecific antibody format, a platform Roche had been developing internally for years. Phesgo was a lifecycle management of the HER2 franchise.
Dealmaking as a growth engine for Roche Back to Top
Since 2023, Roche has deployed significant capital towards dealmaking. At the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in January 2025, Roche Pharmaceutical Division’s CEO Teresa Graham said Roche has roughly $10 billion in M&A firepower it can deploy each year, and is ready to use it “if and when we find transformative assets that are either complementary to our portfolio or change the game in important diseases.”
said
Across different therapeutic areas, two types dominate in Roche’s dealmaking strategy. The first is large-scale acquisitions of late-stage or phase-3-ready assets where the clinical risk is substantially de-risked.
First, Telavant in October 2023 for $7.1 billion upfront , plus a $150 million near-term milestone, giving Roche U.S. and Japan rights to afimkibart, an anti-TL1A antibody with strong phase 2b data in ulcerative colitis and a novel mechanism targeting both inflammation and fibrosis.
$7.1 billion upfront
Six weeks later, Carmot Therapeutics for $2.7 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestones, bringing three clinical-stage obesity candidates, including CT-388, a phase-2-ready dual GLP-1/GIP agonist.
Carmot Therapeutics
Then, 89bio in September 2025, $2.4 billion upfront and up to $3.5 billion total, for pegozafermin, an FGF21 analog in phase 3 for MASH.
89bio
The second deal type is smaller and more targeted. Poseida Therapeutics in November 2024 for up to $1.5 billion, bringing an allogeneic CAR-T platform built on non-viral, T stem cell memory-rich engineering, a technology Roche had already been partnering with since 2022.
Poseida Therapeutics
Regor Therapeutics in September 2024 for $850 million upfront, technically an asset purchase through Genentech, bringing two next-generation CDK inhibitors for breast cancer, positioned as potential successors to the CDK4/6 inhibitor class. And in March 2025, the Zealand Pharma collaboration : $1.65 billion upfront against a total potential value of $5.3 billion to co-develop petrelintide, an amylin analog being positioned as a combination partner for CT-388 in obesity.
Regor Therapeutics
collaboration
These deals represent commitments of well over $15 billion in upfront capital across roughly two years, in IBD, obesity, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), cell therapy, and breast cancer oncology.
How is Roche strategically positioned? Back to Top
The most ambitious expansion Roche has operated in the past few years might be towards obesity. But it has also invested significantly in immunology, as well as neuroscience.
Roche’s obesity pipeline: the most audacious bet
Roche’s obesity pipeline strategy looks like a portfolio bet on where the market will be in five to seven years. The lead asset is CT-388, a once-weekly injectable dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist, the same mechanistic class as Eli Lilly’s Zepbound. In January 2026, Roche published phase 2 data showing placebo-adjusted weight loss of 22.5% at 48 weeks at the highest dose tested, without reaching a plateau. Two phase 3 trials are now underway, initiated in Q1 2026.
published
The timing might not be the most convenient, as CT-388 is entering phase 3 at a point when Wegovy and Zepbound already have years of commercial infrastructure, physician familiarity, and patient uptake behind them. Even if the phase 3 trials deliver, Roche is behind its competition.
Roche’s answer to that timing problem is the combination strategy. The obesity market today is dominated by single-agent GLP-1s, but their key limitation is tolerability, with nausea driving most discontinuation, and concerns about muscle mass loss on long-term treatment. Roche is betting that by the time it arrives, combinations addressing those limitations will be the new standard.
Petrelintide, acquired via the $1.65 billion upfront Zealand Pharma collaboration, is an amylin analog designed to complement GLP-1 therapy by reducing nausea and preserving lean mass, with a combination study with CT-388 planned for the first half of 2026.
However, not all bets can go all the way, and in March 2026, Roche announced it would discontinue Emugrobart, an anti-myostatin antibody meant to address lean mass preservation that was tested in combination with Mounjaro.
announced
Bowel disease and immunology: inside Roche’s $7 billion bet on TL1A
IBD is a large and commercially active market, but it remains one where current therapies fall short of what patients need. Despite a generation of biologics, TNF inhibitors, IL-12/23 antibodies, and integrin blockers, most patients with ulcerative colitis or Crohn’s disease do not achieve lasting remission. This is what made TL1A, a cytokine involved in both inflammation and fibrosis, one of the most closely watched targets in immunology over the past few years.
TL1A is distinct from most existing IBD targets because it drives immune activation in addition to fibrosis, the scarring that causes long-term complications in Crohn’s disease. An antibody that addresses both processes could do more than current biologics. That is the bet behind afimkibart, the anti-TL1A antibody Roche acquired with Telavant. Phase 3 trials in ulcerative colitis are ongoing, with a regulatory filing expected in 2027, and a phase 2 in Crohn’s disease also underway.
However, the TL1A space has become one of the most crowded in immunology: Merck, Sanofi/Teva, AbbVie, Spyre Therapeutics, and Boehringer Ingelheim are all pursuing the target. And in June 2026, Merck announced that its TL1A antibody tulisokibart had delivered the first positive phase 3 results in ulcerative colitis, beating Roche to the clinical milestone. Roche’s afimkibart had been seen as a frontrunner, but Merck has now moved ahead. Roche’s filings are expected in 2027, which means it will likely be a second or third entrant, potentially a different commercial proposition than the $7.1 billion price tag, and making differentiation more important than before.
Cell therapy and neuroscience: Roche’s new bets and unfinished business
The Poseida acquisition gives Roche something it didn’t have: a credible allogeneic CAR-T platform. Current autologous CAR-T therapies require manufacturing a product from each patient’s own cells, which is time-consuming, expensive, and logistically complex. Allogeneic therapies use donor cells manufactured in advance, enabling faster access and potentially lower costs. Poseida’s lead candidate, P-BCMA-ALLO1, targeting multiple myeloma, showed a 91% overall response rate in phase 1 data in heavily pretreated patients. The platform also has applications in autoimmune disease, an area of growing interest.
On the neuroscience side, Roche has a painful history in neurodegeneration with gantenerumab failing in phase 3 Alzheimer’s trials in 2022, following earlier setbacks with crenezumab and other pipeline candidates.
It is in this context that Roche is advancing trontinemab, its next Alzheimer’s candidate. Trontinemab is different from gantenerumab and uses Roche’s proprietary Brainshuttle technology to carry the antibody across the blood-brain barrier via the transferrin receptor, achieving faster and deeper amyloid clearance than prior antibodies. Phase 2 data showed 91% of participants became amyloid PET-negative after six months. Two phase 3 trials have been initiated, but the question is also whether amyloid clearance will translate to cognitive benefit, which is the question that has challenged Alzheimer’s programs before.
showed
Then there’s Roche’s ambiguous Parkinson’s story with Prasinezumab for Parkinson’s. The phase 2 Padova trial missed its primary endpoint, but Roche has committed to phase 3, citing that the totality of data suggests the drug could be the first disease-modifying candidate. That is a defensible call given the near-total absence of disease-modifying options in Parkinson’s , but it also requires the phase 3 to deliver where the phase 2 could not.
committed
Parkinson’s
A second reinvention for Roche Back to Top
Roche navigated the biosimilar cliff by staying close to what it knew: bispecific antibodies, lifecycle management, and next-generation iterations in established biology. The strategy now feels different as the company is stepping out of what it was built on. Obesity, IBD, allogeneic cell therapy, and neurodegeneration are not extensions of an existing franchise. They are new territory and often in markets that are already competitive and, in some cases, already moving faster than Roche anticipated.
The company is well established and has the financial discipline, the infrastructure, and the dealmaking capabilities to compete. But this isn’t proof that the combination thesis in obesity will outpace Lilly and Novo Nordisk’s head start, that afimkibart can differentiate in a TL1A field where Merck has now moved first, or that trontinemab will become the Alzheimer’s success Roche has been looking for.
Roche now looks like a company built on oncology and diagnostics, reinventing itself across several therapeutic areas at once.

articles.title: Antengene Inks ~$1.96B Bispecific T-Cell Engager Deal with K2 Therapeutics
articles.text:
Skip to content Skip to footer NEWS Antengene Inks ~$1.96B Bispecific T-Cell Engager Deal with K2 Therapeutics By Ridhi Rastogi 2 days ago Ink & Toner Shots: Antengene has licensed ATG-106, a preclinical CDH6×CD3 bispecific T-cell engager for solid tumors, to K2 Therapeutics & granted an option for exclusive rights to a second undisclosed bispecific TCE candidate, covering all global markets excl. Greater China As per the licensing deal, Antengene will receive $20M in upfront & near-term consideration, incl. cash & a minority equity stake in a K2’s subsidiary, plus ~$960.5M in development, regulatory, & sales milestones, with tiered royalties on future net sales. Upon exercise of the option, Antengene will receive ~$20M, incl. an option exercise fee, upfront, & a minority equity stake in the related asset company. articles.title: Ophthalmology venture grabs investors' attention, raises $330 million
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Biotech Ophthalmology venture grabs investors’ attention, raises $330 million Ollin Biosciences is developing treatments for eye diseases Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article An eye with a normal retina (left) vs. an eye presenting signs of macular edema, for which Ollin Biosciences is developing a treatment. Adobe By Allison DeAngelis June 24, 2026 Biotech Startups and Venture Capital Reporter Allison DeAngelis allison.deangelis@statnews.com Allison covers what hot startups are getting up to, and who’s financing them. She is also co-host of the weekly biotech podcast, “ The Readout Loud .” You can reach Allison on Signal at AllisonDeAngelis.01. Get email alerts for Allison DeAngelis Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts A startup developing treatments for eye diseases has raised $330 million from investors as it readies for late-stage testing. Ollin Biosciences is about to begin a Phase 3 trial of a treatment for both diabetic macular edema and wet age-related macular degeneration. The conditions affect millions of Americans and can lead to vision loss. Advertisement Ahead of the trial, Ollin told STAT it had raised the Series B funding from an assortment of established biotech investors, a pension fund, and even crossover investors — a breed of private investors who will take a stake in a company shortly before it moves over to the stock market. TCGX and ARCH Venture Partners co-led the funding round, which was one of the largest Series B rounds for a biotech company in the last two years. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. A startup developing treatments for eye diseases has raised $330 million from investors as it readies for late-stage testing. Ollin Biosciences is about to begin a Phase 3 trial of a treatment for both diabetic macular edema and wet age-related macular degeneration. The conditions affect millions of Americans and can lead to vision loss. Advertisement Ahead of the trial, Ollin told STAT it had raised the Series B funding from an assortment of established biotech investors, a pension fund, and even crossover investors — a breed of private investors who will take a stake in a company shortly before it moves over to the stock market. TCGX and ARCH Venture Partners co-led the funding round, which was one of the largest Series B rounds for a biotech company in the last two years. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: Sigvotatug Vedotin Does Not Significantly Improve Survival in NSCLC Trial
articles.text:
Advertisement x
Advertisement
x
Advertisement News | Articles | June 23, 2026 Sigvotatug Vedotin Does Not Significantly Improve Survival in NSCLC Trial Author(s) Russ Conroy Fact checked by: Ariana Pelosci Interact with this article Ask Scout! Ask questions, get summaries, explore topics. Instantly. Investigators will share detailed results from the phase 3 SigVie-002 trial assessing sigvotatug vedotin in NSCLC at a future medical meeting. Advertisement “The ability of sigvotatug vedotin to induce immunogenic cell death provides a strong rationale for combination approaches with immunotherapy, particularly in earlier treatment settings where immune competence is better preserved,” according to Solange Peters, MD, PhD. Sigvotatug vedotin, an investigational IB6-directed antibody drug conjugate, did not significantly improve overall survival (OS) vs docetaxel among patients with previously treated locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic nonsquamous non–small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), according to a press release on topline findings from the phase 3 SigVie-002 trial (NCT06012435). 1 Among patients who received at least 1 prior line of therapy across the overall population, sigvotatug vedotin did not fulfill the trial’s primary end point of OS compared with docetaxel. Among those who received 1 prior line of treatment, investigators reported a stronger trend for OS and progression-free survival (PFS) with the experimental agent. Additionally, exploratory analysis findings showed no clear relationship between IB6 expression and responses. The safety profile of sigvotatug vedotin in the phase 3 trial was comparable with prior reports of the agent. Investigators will submit detailed results from the study for presentation at a medical meeting in the future. “It is important not to underestimate the activity of docetaxel as a comparator in this setting. Patients enrolled in this trial were heavily pretreated, with most having previously received both platinum-based chemotherapy and immunotherapy, yet docetaxel continues to provide meaningful clinical benefit,” Solange Peters, MD, PhD, chair of Medical Oncology & Thoracic Cancers Clinic at Lausanne University Hospital in Switzerland, stated in the press release. 1 “Although the study did not meet its [OS] end point, in second-line patients the data suggest a clinically meaningful survival benefit for sigvotatug vedotin over docetaxel, supporting continued scientific evaluation of sigvotatug vedotin in earlier lines in combination with immunotherapy.” Investigators of the ongoing SigVie-002 trial are evaluating sigvotatug vedotin vs docetaxel among adult patients with previously treated locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic NSCLC. Patients were randomly assigned to receive sigvotatug vedotin intravenously on days 1 and 15 of each 28-day cycle or intravenous docetaxel on day 1 of each cycle. The trial’s primary end point was OS. Secondary end points included PFS per RECIST v1.1 criteria, objective response rate, duration of response, adverse effects, and global health status or quality of life. 2 Patients 18 years and older with histologically or cytologically confirmed stage IIIB, IIIC, M1a, M1b, or M1c NSCLC per AJCC v8.0 guidelines and nonsquamous histology were eligible for enrollment on the trial. Other eligibility criteria included having measurable disease per RECIST v1.1 guidelines; an ECOG performance status of 0 or 1; and adequate hematologic, hepatic, and renal function at baseline. Those with a life expectancy of less than 3 months, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, and pre-existing peripheral neuropathy of grade 2 or higher were ineligible for study entry. Having active central nervous system lesions, including leptomeningeal metastasis, was also grounds for exclusion from the trial. According to the press release, developers are currently assessing sigvotatug vedotin across multiple disease stages and patient populations with NSCLC and other solid tumors. An ongoing phase 3 study is assessing sigvotatug vedotin plus pembrolizumab (Keytruda) among those with frontline NSCLC and a PD-L1 tumor proportion score of at least 50%. Additionally, investigators are evaluating the agent in combination with PF’4404, a novel bispecific antibody designed to target VEGF and PD-L1, in early-stage lung cancer and other tumors that express IB6. “The ability of sigvotatug vedotin to induce immunogenic cell death provides a strong rationale for combination approaches with immunotherapy, particularly in earlier treatment settings where immune competence is better preserved. In this context, the promising phase 1 efficacy signals observed in patients [who are treatment naive] with high PD-L1 expression warrant further evaluation and may represent a more effective clinical application of this strategy,” Peters concluded. 1 References Pfizer announces topline phase 3 results for sigvotatug vedotin in previously treated metastatic non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer. News release. Pfizer. June 22, 2026. Accessed June 23, 2026. https://tinyurl.com/mrezz6vs A study of SGN-B6A versus docetaxel in previously treated non-small cell lung cancer. ClinicalTrials.gov. Updated May 27, 2026. Accessed June 23, 2026. https://tinyurl.com/4ecc3nw2 Newsletter Stay up to date on recent advances in the multidisciplinary approach to cancer. Sigvotatug vedotin, an investigational IB6-directed antibody drug conjugate, did not significantly improve overall survival (OS) vs docetaxel among patients with previously treated locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic nonsquamous non–small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), according to a press release on topline findings from the phase 3 SigVie-002 trial (NCT06012435). 1 Among patients who received at least 1 prior line of therapy across the overall population, sigvotatug vedotin did not fulfill the trial’s primary end point of OS compared with docetaxel. Among those who received 1 prior line of treatment, investigators reported a stronger trend for OS and progression-free survival (PFS) with the experimental agent. Additionally, exploratory analysis findings showed no clear relationship between IB6 expression and responses. The safety profile of sigvotatug vedotin in the phase 3 trial was comparable with prior reports of the agent. Investigators will submit detailed results from the study for presentation at a medical meeting in the future. “It is important not to underestimate the activity of docetaxel as a comparator in this setting. Patients enrolled in this trial were heavily pretreated, with most having previously received both platinum-based chemotherapy and immunotherapy, yet docetaxel continues to provide meaningful clinical benefit,” Solange Peters, MD, PhD, chair of Medical Oncology & Thoracic Cancers Clinic at Lausanne University Hospital in Switzerland, stated in the press release. 1 “Although the study did not meet its [OS] end point, in second-line patients the data suggest a clinically meaningful survival benefit for sigvotatug vedotin over docetaxel, supporting continued scientific evaluation of sigvotatug vedotin in earlier lines in combination with immunotherapy.” Investigators of the ongoing SigVie-002 trial are evaluating sigvotatug vedotin vs docetaxel among adult patients with previously treated locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic NSCLC. Patients were randomly assigned to receive sigvotatug vedotin intravenously on days 1 and 15 of each 28-day cycle or intravenous docetaxel on day 1 of each cycle. The trial’s primary end point was OS. Secondary end points included PFS per RECIST v1.1 criteria, objective response rate, duration of response, adverse effects, and global health status or quality of life. 2 Patients 18 years and older with histologically or cytologically confirmed stage IIIB, IIIC, M1a, M1b, or M1c NSCLC per AJCC v8.0 guidelines and nonsquamous histology were eligible for enrollment on the trial. Other eligibility criteria included having measurable disease per RECIST v1.1 guidelines; an ECOG performance status of 0 or 1; and adequate hematologic, hepatic, and renal function at baseline. Those with a life expectancy of less than 3 months, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, and pre-existing peripheral neuropathy of grade 2 or higher were ineligible for study entry. Having active central nervous system lesions, including leptomeningeal metastasis, was also grounds for exclusion from the trial. According to the press release, developers are currently assessing sigvotatug vedotin across multiple disease stages and patient populations with NSCLC and other solid tumors. An ongoing phase 3 study is assessing sigvotatug vedotin plus pembrolizumab (Keytruda) among those with frontline NSCLC and a PD-L1 tumor proportion score of at least 50%. Additionally, investigators are evaluating the agent in combination with PF’4404, a novel bispecific antibody designed to target VEGF and PD-L1, in early-stage lung cancer and other tumors that express IB6. “The ability of sigvotatug vedotin to induce immunogenic cell death provides a strong rationale for combination approaches with immunotherapy, particularly in earlier treatment settings where immune competence is better preserved. In this context, the promising phase 1 efficacy signals observed in patients [who are treatment naive] with high PD-L1 expression warrant further evaluation and may represent a more effective clinical application of this strategy,” Peters concluded. 1 References Pfizer announces topline phase 3 results for sigvotatug vedotin in previously treated metastatic non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer. Sigvotatug Vedotin Does Not Significantly Improve Survival in NSCLC Trial
Author(s) Russ Conroy
Author(s)
Russ Conroy
Fact checked by: Ariana Pelosci
Fact checked by:
Ariana Pelosci
